section.title: The Queen of Sheba
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 9 closed with the LORD appearing to Solomon a second time, warning that the throne stands or falls on covenant faithfulness. Chapter 10 shows Solomon's glory at its height: a foreign queen travels a great distance to test his famous wisdom, finds the report more than true, and blesses the LORD for setting him on Israel's throne. The chapter then lingers over Solomon's staggering wealth, gold, ivory, cedar, chariots, and horses flowing in year after year. It is the summit of the story, and the very list of riches quietly gathers the ingredients of the fall that chapter 11 will describe.
section.thesis: At the height of his reign, Solomon's God-given wisdom draws a foreign queen to bless the LORD, yet the towering wealth surrounding him, gold, horses, and the beginnings of many wives, is exactly what the law warned a king not to multiply for himself, so that the chapter's glory already carries the seeds of chapter 11's decline, and points beyond Solomon to the one of whom Jesus said, something greater than Solomon is here.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the queen of Sheba comes to test Solomon with hard questions and is overwhelmed, verses 1 to 5; she blesses the LORD for what she has seen and exchanges gifts with the king, verses 6 to 13; Solomon's fabulous wealth, throne, and household described in detail, verses 14 to 22; and Solomon's fame, tribute, and multiplying horses and chariots surpassing every king of the earth, verses 23 to 29.

units.label: The queen comes to test him
units.note: wisdom that took her breath away

verses.text: Now when the queen of Sheba heard about the fame of Solomon concerning the {{shem|name}} of the LORD, she came to test him with difficult questions.
She arrived in Jerusalem with a very large caravan, with camels bearing spices, gold in great abundance, and precious stones. And she came to Solomon and spoke to him all that was on her mind.
And Solomon answered all her questions; nothing was too difficult for the king to explain.
When the queen of Sheba saw all the wisdom of Solomon, the palace he had built,
the food at his table, the seating of his servants, the service and attire of his attendants, his cupbearers, and the burnt offerings he presented at the house of the LORD, it took her breath away.

units.label: Blessed be the LORD your God
units.note: a foreign queen confesses the true King

verses.text: She said to the king, “The report I heard in my own country about your words and wisdom is true.
But I did not believe these things until I came and saw with my own eyes. Indeed, not even half was told to me. Your wisdom and prosperity have far exceeded the report I heard.
How blessed are your men! How blessed are these servants of yours who stand continually before you and hear your wisdom!

{{baruk|Blessed}} be the LORD your God, who has delighted in you to set you on the throne of Israel. Because of the LORD's eternal love for Israel, He has made you king to carry out justice and righteousness.”
Then she gave the king 120 talents of gold, a great quantity of spices, and precious stones. Never again were spices in such abundance brought in as those the queen of Sheba gave to King Solomon.
(The fleet of Hiram that brought gold from Ophir also brought from Ophir a great cargo of almug wood and precious stones.

The king made the almug wood into steps for the house of the LORD and for the king's palace, and into lyres and harps for the singers. Never before had such almug wood been brought in, nor has such been seen again to this day.)

King Solomon gave the queen of Sheba all she desired, whatever she asked, besides what he had given her out of his royal bounty. Then she left and returned to her own country, along with her servants.
The weight of gold that came to Solomon each year was 666 talents,
not including the revenue from the merchants, traders, and all the Arabian kings and governors of the land.
King Solomon made two hundred large shields of hammered gold; six hundred shekels of gold went into each shield.
He also made three hundred small shields of hammered gold; three minas of gold went into each shield. And the king put them in the House of the Forest of Lebanon.
Additionally, the king made a great throne of ivory and overlaid it with pure gold.
The throne had six steps, and its back had a rounded top. There were armrests on both sides of the seat, with a lion standing beside each armrest.
Twelve lions stood on the six steps, one at either end of each step. Nothing like this had ever been made for any kingdom.
All King Solomon's drinking cups were gold, and all the utensils of the House of the Forest of Lebanon were pure gold. There was no silver, because it was accounted as nothing in the days of Solomon.
For the king had the ships of Tarshish at sea with Hiram's fleet, and once every three years the ships of Tarshish would arrive bearing gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks.

units.label: Surpassing all the kings of the earth
units.note: the wisdom God put in his heart

verses.text: So King Solomon surpassed all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom.
The whole world sought an audience with Solomon to hear the {{chokmah|wisdom}} that God had put in his {{lev|heart}}.
Year after year, each visitor would bring his tribute: articles of silver and gold, clothing, weapons, spices, horses, and mules.
Solomon accumulated 1,400 chariots and 12,000 horses, which he stationed in the chariot cities and also with him in Jerusalem.
The king made silver as common in Jerusalem as stones, and cedar as abundant as sycamore in the foothills.
Solomon's horses were imported from Egypt and Kue; the royal merchants purchased them from Kue.
A chariot could be imported from Egypt for six hundred shekels of silver, and a horse for a hundred and fifty. Likewise, they exported them to all the kings of the Hittites and to the kings of Aram.

shem.gloss: Name, reputation, fame. The queen of Sheba comes because of the shem of the LORD, His fame reaching her land through Solomon; her long journey is set in motion by reports of God's own name, not merely Solomon's.

baruk.gloss: Blessed, a passive participle used to praise God or invoke favor. The queen's confession, Blessed be the LORD your God, is a foreign ruler's own mouth crediting Israel's God, not Solomon, for the throne and its justice.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, skill in living rightly and ruling well, a gift God gave Solomon at Gibeon, 1 Kings 3:9-12. The whole chapter turns on this chokmah, sought out by a queen from the ends of the earth and later cited by Jesus Himself, Matthew 12:42.

lev.gloss: Heart, the inner seat of mind and will. Verse 24 says God put wisdom in Solomon's lev; the very next chapter reports that his many wives turned that same heart after other gods, showing how quickly a heart gifted with wisdom can still be led astray.

basic.context: Solomon's reign is at its peak. A queen from the distant land of Sheba, hearing of his fame, travels to Jerusalem to test him with hard questions, and finds that his wisdom and wealth exceed everything she had been told. She blesses the LORD for setting Solomon on Israel's throne, and the two exchange lavish gifts. The chapter then describes Solomon's riches in detail: gold beyond counting, an ivory throne overlaid with gold, ships bringing exotic cargo, and a growing fleet of chariots and horses. The key questions the chapter raises are what the queen's visit and blessing signify for the nations, and why the narrator lingers so long over gold, horses, and trade just before chapter 11 records Solomon's fall.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative reporting a real visit and a real accumulation of wealth; it is not a promise that wisdom always produces wealth, nor a simple celebration of riches. Read alongside Deuteronomy 17:16-17, where the king is explicitly told not to multiply horses, wives, or silver and gold for himself, the chapter's admiring tone sits in tension with the law, and readers are meant to notice that Solomon is quietly doing exactly what a king was warned against, just before the account of his heart being turned away.
basic.originalLanguages: The queen comes because of the shem, the name, of the LORD tied to Solomon's chokmah, wisdom, verse 1. She blesses the LORD, using the participle baruk, blessed, crediting Him rather than Solomon for the throne, verse 9. And the chapter closes by noting the chokmah God had put in Solomon's lev, his heart, verse 24, the same heart that chapter 11 says his many wives will turn away.
basic.typology: The queen of Sheba, a Gentile ruler who travels from the ends of the earth to sit under Solomon's wisdom and departs in awe, foreshadows the nations streaming to a wisdom greater than Solomon's. The tribute and gifts brought to Solomon by kings and traders anticipate Psalm 72's vision of the kings of Sheba bringing gifts to God's true king, fulfilled when the Magi bring gold to the infant Christ.
basic.christ: Jesus explicitly points His hearers back to this chapter: the Queen of the South will rise at the judgment and condemn that generation, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear Solomon's wisdom, and something greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42. Where the queen crossed a vast distance to test a man's wisdom and found it exceeded the report, Christ is the wisdom of God in person, 1 Corinthians 1:24, who welcomes the nations and, unlike Solomon, never lets His heart be turned away.
basic.god: God is the true source of Solomon's wisdom and the one who set him on the throne to carry out justice and righteousness, as even a foreign queen recognizes and confesses. The chapter shows God delighting to bless a king who reflects His own character, while the accumulating gold, horses, and trade quietly show how easily good gifts, enjoyed apart from continued obedience, become the very things a king was warned not to gather for himself.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 17:16-17 warns the king against multiplying horses, wives, and silver and gold, the very list this chapter describes Solomon amassing. Psalm 72 prays that the kings of Sheba would bring gifts to God's righteous king. Matthew 12:42 has Jesus cite the queen of the South as proof that something greater than Solomon has now come. And 1 Corinthians 1:24 names Christ Himself as the wisdom of God.
basic.oneStory: Solomon's reign reaches its summit here, wisdom and wealth drawing even a foreign queen to bless the LORD, a foretaste of the nations one day streaming to God's true king. But the same chapter that displays the height of glory quietly gathers the gold, the horses, and the beginnings of many wives that the law had warned against, showing that blessing without watchfulness becomes a snare. The whole story presses forward to Jesus, the true and better Solomon, greater in wisdom, whose heart never turns.

context.literary: The chapter is built as a showcase, moving from the queen's testing questions and overwhelmed response to a sweeping catalog of Solomon's wealth, then closing with a summary claim that he surpassed every king of the earth. The admiring, almost breathless tone of the inventory, gold, ivory, cedar, ships, chariots, mirrors the queen's own breathlessness in verse 5, letting the reader feel the same dazzling excess before chapter 11 turns it into warning.
context.historical: Sheba was likely located in south Arabia, modern Yemen, a kingdom that controlled valuable incense and spice trade routes; a royal visit combining diplomacy, trade, and the testing of a foreign ruler's reputation for wisdom was a recognized practice in the ancient Near East. Ophir's gold, almug wood, and the ships of Tarshish reflect Solomon's extensive maritime trade networks built through his alliance with Hiram of Tyre, first described in chapters 5 and 9.
context.canonical: This chapter is the high water mark of the Solomon narrative, immediately followed in chapter 11 by his decline, so that the two chapters must be read together: the very items catalogued here, gold, horses, and foreign alliances sealed through marriage, are what Deuteronomy 17 had already flagged as dangers for any king of Israel. Jesus later treats the queen's journey as a paradigm for how the nations will respond to a wisdom greater than Solomon's, Matthew 12:42.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To display Solomon's God-given wisdom and wealth at its height, showing that even a foreign queen recognized the LORD's hand behind it, while positioning the details of that wealth so that a reader familiar with the law can already sense the warning signs of the fall to come in chapter 11.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical visit and a specific accumulation of wealth; it does not prescribe that wisdom guarantees riches or that riches are automatically wrong. What endures is the pattern: God's gifts are meant to be received with continued obedience, and even legitimate blessing gathered without restraint can become the very thing scripture had already warned against.

debates.question: Was the queen of Sheba's visit primarily a spiritual response to God, or a diplomatic and trade mission?

views.label: A genuine recognition of the LORD
views.case: Her own words, Blessed be the LORD your God, verse 9, credit Israel's God rather than Solomon, and Jesus later treats her journey as a model of someone who sought out true wisdom, Matthew 12:42, suggesting real spiritual insight rather than mere flattery.

views.label: Primarily political and commercial diplomacy
views.case: The chapter frames her arrival around camels bearing spices, gold, and precious stones, verse 2, and closes with an extensive exchange of royal gifts, verses 10 and 13, consistent with the trade diplomacy common between kingdoms that controlled valuable trade routes, such as Sheba's incense trade.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text records a Gentile ruler's own mouth acknowledging the LORD's hand in Solomon's reign, so that even a mission driven by trade becomes an occasion for the nations to glimpse the true God, anticipating both Israel's calling to the nations and Christ's greater wisdom that draws people from every corner of the earth.

debates.question: Do the gold, horses, and wealth catalogued in verses 14 to 29 read as straightforward praise or as an implicit warning?

views.label: Straightforward praise of God's blessing
views.case: The passage reports plainly that Solomon surpassed all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom, verse 23, with no explicit criticism attached within the chapter itself, presenting his wealth as the fruit of the wisdom God had given him.

views.label: An implicit warning building toward chapter 11
views.case: Deuteronomy 17:16-17 explicitly forbids the king from multiplying horses, wives, and silver and gold for himself, and this chapter methodically reports Solomon doing all three, immediately before chapter 11 opens with his many wives turning his heart away.

debates.bearing: Reading chapters 10 and 11 as one movement, the narrator lets the numbers speak for themselves: the very glory celebrated here is quietly sowing what chapter 11 will reap, teaching that blessing enjoyed apart from continued obedience to God's word becomes a snare rather than a security.

study.originalLanguages: The queen travels because of the shem, the name, of the LORD joined to Solomon's chokmah, wisdom, verse 1, and finds that not even half of it had been told to her. She blesses the LORD, opening her praise with baruk, blessed, a word that credits God rather than the king for the throne and its justice, verse 9. The chapter closes noting the chokmah God had placed in Solomon's lev, his heart, verse 24, the very heart that the next chapter will say his many wives turned away after other gods, so that the vocabulary of this chapter already anticipates the vocabulary of the next.

typology.type: The queen of Sheba traveling from the ends of the earth to test Solomon's wisdom
typology.fulfillment: The nations streaming to Christ, the greater wisdom of God, of whom Jesus said, something greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42, 1 Corinthians 1:24.

typology.type: Kings and traders bringing tribute of gold and spices to Solomon
typology.fulfillment: Psalm 72's vision of the kings of Sheba bringing gifts to God's true and righteous king, echoed when the Magi bring gold to the infant Christ, Matthew 2:11.

typology.type: A king surpassing all others in wisdom yet secretly accumulating what the law forbade
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true King who is Himself full of wisdom and never departs from the Father's will, unlike every earthly king before Him, Colossians 2:3.

study.christ: Jesus Himself reaches back to this chapter: the Queen of the South will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here, Matthew 12:42. The queen's long journey and overwhelmed response, verse 5, becomes the measure Jesus uses for how seriously the nations should seek out true wisdom. Solomon was wise, but his heart was later turned; Christ is Himself the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, Colossians 2:3, who welcomes the nations, as Solomon did the queen, but who never fails in faithfulness the way Solomon soon will.
study.god: God is the giver of Solomon's wisdom and the one who set him on Israel's throne to carry out justice and righteousness, a fact even a foreign queen confesses with her own lips, verse 9. He delights to bless obedience and to draw the nations to Himself through it. Yet the same narrative that displays His generous blessing lets the careful reader see, in the sheer accumulation of gold, horses, and foreign alliances, exactly what His law had warned a king not to gather for himself, showing that God's gifts are never meant to be enjoyed apart from continued trust in His word.

crossReferences.note: The king is commanded not to multiply horses, wives, or silver and gold for himself, the very list this chapter reports Solomon accumulating.
A prayer that the kings of Sheba and other nations would bring tribute and gifts to God's true and righteous king.
Jesus cites the queen of the South's journey to hear Solomon's wisdom as proof that something greater than Solomon has come.
Christ is named as the wisdom of God, the reality toward which Solomon's God-given wisdom always pointed.

study.oneStory: Solomon's reign reaches its high point here: wisdom and wealth so great that a queen crosses a vast distance to test it and leaves blessing the LORD, a small foretaste of the nations one day streaming to God's true king. But the very chapter that shows this glory is quietly gathering the gold, the horses, and the beginnings of many foreign wives that the law had already warned a king not to multiply for himself. The story is pressing on toward Jesus, the true and better Solomon, in whom is found a wisdom that never fades and a heart that never turns.

quiz.q: Why did the queen of Sheba travel to Jerusalem to see Solomon?

options.text: She heard about the fame of Solomon concerning the name of the LORD and came to test him with difficult questions, v1.
options.note: This is the chapter's own stated reason, tying her journey directly to the LORD's name and Solomon's wisdom.

options.text: She came mainly to arrange a marriage alliance with Solomon.
options.note: Gift exchange and diplomacy are present in the chapter, but no marriage is mentioned; the stated purpose is to test his wisdom.

options.text: She was fleeing conflict in her own country and needed refuge.
options.note: Nothing in the text suggests she was fleeing anything; she came deliberately to test Solomon.

options.text: She had never heard of Solomon and stumbled upon Jerusalem by accident.
options.note: Verse 1 says she came because she had heard of his fame; it was intentional, not accidental.

quiz.q: What did the queen of Sheba confess after seeing Solomon's wisdom and wealth?

options.text: That the LORD, not Solomon alone, is to be blessed for delighting in him and setting him on Israel's throne to do justice and righteousness, v9.
options.note: Her own words in verse 9 credit the LORD directly for Solomon's throne and reign.

options.text: That Solomon's wealth alone impressed her, with no comment on the LORD.
options.note: She is deeply impressed by his wealth, but she explicitly names and blesses the LORD, not merely admiring riches.

options.text: That she doubted the reports were true even after seeing everything.
options.note: She says the opposite: not even half was told to her, and the report fell short of reality, v7.

options.text: That she wished to remain in Jerusalem permanently.
options.note: Verse 13 says she returned to her own country; there is no indication she stayed.

quiz.q: According to Deuteronomy 17:16-17, what was a king of Israel explicitly warned not to multiply for himself?

options.text: Horses, wives, and silver and gold, the very three things this chapter shows Solomon accumulating in abundance.
options.note: Deuteronomy 17:16-17 names all three, and 1 Kings 10 catalogs Solomon gathering exactly these.

options.text: Only foreign alliances through trade, with no mention of wealth or horses.
options.note: Trade and alliance are part of it, but the law's specific warning names horses, wives, and silver and gold directly.

options.text: Only the practice of building temples to foreign gods.
options.note: That specific sin appears later in chapter 11, but Deuteronomy 17:16-17 addresses horses, wives, and wealth, not temple building.

options.text: Nothing; the law placed no restrictions on a king's wealth.
options.note: Deuteronomy 17:16-17 gives explicit restrictions that this chapter shows Solomon exceeding.

quiz.q: How does Jesus use the queen of Sheba's visit in Matthew 12:42?

options.text: He cites her as proof that the nations sought true wisdom from a distance, and declares that something greater than Solomon is now present in Himself.
options.note: Matthew 12:42 has Jesus point directly to this chapter to show His own greater wisdom and authority.

options.text: He uses her only as an example of pagan idolatry to be avoided.
options.note: Jesus commends her seeking, using her positively as a contrast to His unbelieving hearers, not as a warning against idolatry.

options.text: He never refers to the queen of Sheba anywhere in the Gospels.
options.note: Matthew 12:42 explicitly names the Queen of the South and her journey to hear Solomon's wisdom.

options.text: He claims she never truly believed anything about the LORD.
options.note: Jesus commends her, using her example to rebuke those who reject a greater wisdom now present.

quiz.q: Why does the chapter's admiring inventory of gold, horses, and wealth (vv14-29) matter for reading chapter 11 rightly?

options.text: It quietly reports Solomon doing exactly what the law warned a king not to do, so that the glory of chapter 10 already contains the seeds of the decline in chapter 11.
options.note: Read against Deuteronomy 17:16-17, the numbers themselves foreshadow the coming fall.

options.text: It shows that wealth had nothing to do with Solomon's later unfaithfulness.
options.note: The text draws a direct line from foreign wives and multiplied wealth to the heart that chapter 11 says was turned away.

options.text: It proves that riches are always sinful regardless of the source.
options.note: The chapter treats wealth as a genuine gift from God; the danger lies in multiplying it apart from obedience, not in wealth itself.

options.text: It has no connection at all to the events of chapter 11.
options.note: The two chapters are deliberately placed together, with chapter 10's wealth setting up chapter 11's account of the heart turned away.

questions: The queen of Sheba crossed a great distance to test Solomon's wisdom and found it exceeded every report. What are you willing to go out of your way to seek when it comes to knowing God's wisdom?
She blessed the LORD, not Solomon, for what she saw. Where might you be tempted to admire a gift, a leader, or an achievement without giving God the credit that belongs to Him?

The very wealth that crowned Solomon's glory, gold, horses, and multiplying alliances, was what the law had already warned a king not to gather. Where in your own life is a good gift quietly becoming something you are gathering apart from obedience?
Jesus said something greater than Solomon is here. How does knowing that Christ Himself is the wisdom of God change where you go first with your hardest questions?
Solomon's heart, though gifted with wisdom, was not shielded from being turned away. What practices keep your own heart anchored to the LORD in seasons of blessing and success?

translationNotes.note: The queen's caravan of camels bearing spices, gold, and precious stones signals both the wealth of Sheba's trade routes and the scale of the diplomatic gift she intended to bring.
Almug wood, also called algum in 2 Chronicles 2:8, was an imported timber prized for fine construction and instruments; its rarity underscores the extravagance of Solomon's building projects.

Ships of Tarshish can denote either vessels bound for the distant port of Tarshish or, as the BSB footnote allows, simply a class of large trading ships; either reading points to Solomon's extensive maritime trade.

The chariot and horse trade with the Hittites and Aram shows Solomon functioning as a middleman in international commerce, wealth built on exactly the kind of foreign entanglement Deuteronomy 17 had warned against.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 10:1-29. Em dashes in the source (verses 2, 13, 25, 26 footnote wording) were converted to commas or removed; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in this file.
